title: GitLab Values
content:
GitLab's six core values are Collaboration, Results, Efficiency, Diversity Inclusion and Belonging, Iteration, and Transparency, together forming the acronym CREDIT. These values guide all aspects of how the company operates.

Collaboration: Kindness is fundamental to collaboration. We assume positive intent and default to transparency. We share information broadly and proactively. We help others even when it's not our direct responsibility. We say thanks when someone helps us. We give feedback effectively and accept feedback graciously. We address behavior, but we don't label people. We say sorry freely and genuinely. We are open to new ideas, even when they challenge our current approach. We don't pull rank. People are not their work. You are not your code. We practice short toes to minimize territorial behavior. Everyone can contribute.

Results: We measure results, not hours. We care about what you achieve, not how long you sit at your desk. We give agency to team members: You most likely know best how to accomplish your work. We set ambitious goals. We write things down, but we bias toward action. We hold each other accountable and follow through on commitments. We accept that mistakes happen and focus on learning from them. We focus on growth and development, both individual and company-wide.

Efficiency: We care about working on the right things, not on working hard on the wrong things. We write things down to avoid repeated explanations. We use boring solutions — we prefer simple, proven technology over exciting unproven alternatives. We keep artifacts, processes, and policies as simple as possible. We embrace iteration to reduce waste. We practice self-service and self-learning. We are frugal and spend company money as if it were our own. We prefer asynchronous communication over meetings. We aim for the minimum viable change (MVC).

Diversity, Inclusion and Belonging: We foster an environment where everyone can thrive regardless of gender, sexual orientation, race, ethnicity, age, disability, or any other identity. We build a safe community and actively combat bias. We are inclusive in our language and behavior. We seek diverse perspectives in decision-making. We support team members from all backgrounds. We measure belonging and act on feedback to improve.

Iteration: We do the smallest thing possible and get it out as quickly as possible. We aim for minimal viable change (MVC). Iteration means reducing the scope of what you're working on, not the quality. We don't wait for something to be perfect to ship it. We accept that things might be slightly broken along the way. We set a due date rather than a scope for deliverables when possible. We keep merge requests small. We avoid premature optimization and big-bang releases.

Transparency: We share as much information as possible by default. We make things public unless there's a specific reason not to. Our handbook is publicly accessible and we develop it in public. We share company financials with team members. We record meetings and share them. We give access broadly by default and restrict only when necessary. We hold public retrospectives. We are transparent about what we're working on and what decisions we've made.

title: GitLab Communication
content:
GitLab has a strong emphasis on asynchronous communication. As an all-remote company with team members in over 65 countries, we cannot rely on real-time meetings for most communication.

Internal Communication Guidelines: We use GitLab Issues and Merge Requests as the primary tools for communication about work. Everything should start with a merge request. We prefer text-based, asynchronous communication over synchronous meetings. We document decisions in issues or merge requests, not in Slack messages. When in doubt, write it down. If it's not in the handbook, it doesn't exist.

Slack is used for informal communication and quick questions, but decisions and important information should not live solely in Slack. Use the appropriate channel. Be mindful that not everyone is in the same timezone. External stakeholder communications should follow the established brand guidelines.

Meeting Guidelines: We prefer async over sync. If a meeting is needed, have an agenda linked before the meeting. Document the outcome after the meeting. Meetings should start on time, end on time, and have as few attendees as possible. It's OK to decline meetings if there's no agenda. All meetings should be recorded when possible. We use a 25/50 minute meeting format to allow breaks between meetings.

Writing Style: We write in plain language. We avoid jargon when possible. We use inclusive language. We link to single sources of truth (the handbook or documentation). We prefer specificity over vagueness. We write for a global audience. We number our lists when order matters and bullet them when it doesn't.

Everyone Can Contribute: Communication at GitLab follows our value of transparency. We encourage everyone to contribute to the handbook, documentation, and processes. You don't need permission to make improvements. If you see something that needs updating, open a merge request.

Confidential Information: While we default to transparency, some information must remain confidential: personal information about team members, specific legal matters, security vulnerabilities before they're patched, and competitive information. When in doubt, check with your manager or the legal team.

title: All-Remote Guide
content:
GitLab is one of the world's largest all-remote companies with over 2,000 team members across 65+ countries. We have no company-owned offices. All-remote means every team member works remotely — there is no headquarters.

All-Remote vs. Remote-Friendly: All-remote is fundamentally different from remote-friendly. In a remote-friendly company, some people are in offices and some work remotely, creating an uneven playing field. In an all-remote company, everyone is on equal footing because there is no office culture to exclude remote workers.

Benefits of All-Remote: Hiring isn't constrained by geography, allowing access to the best global talent. Team members have increased flexibility in their work environment and schedule. No commute means time savings and environmental benefits. Operating costs are lower without office leases. Results and output are prioritized over presenteeism. Documentation and asynchronous workflows are necessities, leading to better institutional knowledge.

Challenges of All-Remote: Combating loneliness and isolation. Building relationships without in-person interaction. Managing across time zones. Maintaining work-life boundaries. Onboarding new team members. Ensuring equitable access to information.

How GitLab Makes All-Remote Work: Handbook-first approach — all processes and policies are documented publicly. Asynchronous communication as the default. Intentional informal communication through coffee chats, social calls, and virtual events. Results-oriented work culture focused on output, not hours. Strong emphasis on written documentation. Regular team events and in-person gatherings (Contribute). Investment in home office setup for all team members.

Mental Health and Wellbeing: We encourage team members to prioritize mental health. Take time off when needed. Set boundaries between work and personal time. Use the Modern Health benefit for therapy and coaching. Disconnect regularly. It's OK to have a non-linear workday.

All-Remote Certifications and Resources: GitLab offers an all-remote certification course. We share our remote playbook publicly to help other organizations transition to remote work. We contribute to research and thought leadership on remote work.

title: All-Remote Tips
content:
Tips for working effectively in GitLab's all-remote environment.

Design Your Workspace: Create a dedicated workspace if possible. Invest in a quality chair, desk, and monitor. Ensure good lighting for video calls. Consider a standing desk for variety. Keep your workspace clean and organized.

Manage Your Time: Use time blocking to protect deep work periods. Be explicit about your working hours in your calendar. Take regular breaks — use the Pomodoro technique or similar methods. Protect your personal time by setting firm end-of-day boundaries. Use asynchronous communication to work when you're most productive.

Stay Connected: Schedule coffee chats with team members you don't normally work with. Join social Slack channels that interest you. Participate in GitLab team events. Be visible — contribute to discussions, share updates, and engage with others' work. Use video during calls when comfortable to build stronger connections.

Communication Best Practices: Over-communicate context in written messages. Use screenshots and screen recordings to explain complex topics. Be explicit about deadlines and expectations. Assume positive intent in all communications. Respond to messages within a reasonable timeframe for your timezone. Use emoji reactions to acknowledge messages without creating noise.

Productivity Tips: Limit notifications during focus time. Batch similar tasks together. Write things down — your future self will thank you. Use GitLab's own tools (issues, boards, epics) to track your work. Don't let perfect be the enemy of good — ship iteratively.

Onboarding Remotely: Pair with your onboarding buddy frequently. Don't hesitate to ask questions — there are no dumb questions. Record your onboarding journey to help future hires. Contribute to improving the onboarding process as you go through it.

title: All-Remote Meetings
content:
Meetings at GitLab are designed to be efficient, inclusive, and respectful of team members' time.

Meeting Culture: Meetings are expensive — they cost the combined hourly rate of every attendee. We should always ask: Can this be done async instead? If a meeting is necessary, keep it focused and time-boxed. Every meeting must have an agenda shared in advance. No agenda, no meeting.

Meeting Formats: We use 25-minute and 50-minute meeting slots to allow 5-10 minute breaks between meetings. Recurring meetings should have a standing agenda document. One-on-ones between managers and reports happen weekly and are driven by the report's agenda. Group meetings should designate a note-taker and have clear action items.

Async Alternatives to Meetings: Use GitLab Issues for decision-making discussions. Use Merge Requests for document and code reviews. Use recorded video (Loom or similar) for presentations that don't require live interaction. Use Slack threads for quick discussions. Use handbook updates for policy changes.

Inclusive Meeting Practices: Consider time zones when scheduling — rotate meeting times for recurring meetings with global participants. Always provide a way to contribute asynchronously for those who cannot attend live. Record all meetings and share recordings. Use live captioning when available. Speak clearly and at a moderate pace for non-native speakers.

Key Meeting Types: Team meetings, one-on-ones, skip-level meetings, retrospectives, demos, and AMAs (Ask Me Anything). Each has specific guidelines in the handbook. CEO hosts regular AMA sessions open to all team members.

title: All-Remote Management
content:
Managing in an all-remote environment requires intentional practices and a shift from traditional management approaches.

Trust and Autonomy: Managers at GitLab lead by trusting their team members. We manage by results, not by presence. Micromanagement is incompatible with our values. Give clear expectations and the autonomy to meet them. Measure output and impact, not hours worked.

One-on-Ones: Weekly one-on-ones are essential for all managers. The meeting belongs to the direct report — they set the agenda. Use one-on-ones to remove blockers, provide feedback, discuss career development, and check on wellbeing. Document topics and action items in a shared document. Be consistent — don't cancel one-on-ones.

Feedback and Performance: Give feedback regularly, not just during review cycles. Be specific and actionable in feedback. Use the SBI framework: Situation, Behavior, Impact. Create a psychologically safe environment where feedback is welcomed. Address underperformance early and directly with compassion.

Team Building: Intentionally create opportunities for informal interaction. Budget for team events and gatherings. Encourage cross-team collaboration. Celebrate wins publicly. Support team members' growth and development. Be vulnerable as a leader — share your own challenges and learnings.

Hiring Remote Managers: Look for strong written communication skills. Assess ability to build trust without physical presence. Evaluate comfort with asynchronous workflows. Check for empathy and emotional intelligence. Ensure they can balance accountability with autonomy.

title: Informal Communication
content:
Informal communication is critical in an all-remote company because it replaces the organic social interactions that happen naturally in offices.

Coffee Chats: GitLab team members are encouraged to schedule coffee chats — 25-minute video calls with someone from a different team. These are purely social: no agenda, no work topics required. The Donut bot in Slack can pair people randomly for coffee chats. New hires are especially encouraged to use coffee chats to meet colleagues across the company.

Social Slack Channels: GitLab maintains numerous social channels for interests like cooking, fitness, pets, parenting, gaming, music, and more. These channels help team members connect over shared interests. Participation is voluntary and encouraged.

Virtual Team Events: Teams organize virtual social events like game nights, cooking sessions, trivia, and show-and-tell. The company sponsors virtual events for holidays and milestones. These events are optional but help build team cohesion.

Contribute Conference: GitLab's annual in-person event brings team members together for a week of collaboration, learning, and social connection. This is the primary opportunity for face-to-face interaction and is highly valued by team members.

Group Conversations: Some teams use informal group calls or channels for casual check-ins. These might include virtual coworking sessions where team members work quietly together on video. The goal is to replicate the ambient social presence of an office environment.

Why It Matters: Informal communication builds trust, strengthens relationships, and combats the isolation that can come with remote work. It's not a luxury — it's a business necessity. Teams with strong informal connections collaborate more effectively on formal work.

title: Hybrid-Remote
content:
GitLab's perspective on hybrid-remote work models and why all-remote is preferred.

What is Hybrid-Remote: Hybrid-remote refers to organizations where some employees work in offices and others work remotely. This has become common after the COVID-19 pandemic, with many companies adopting hybrid models.

Challenges of Hybrid-Remote: Information asymmetry — office workers often have access to hallway conversations and informal decisions that remote workers miss. Two-tier culture can develop where office workers are seen as more committed or are promoted more frequently. Meeting dynamics favor those in the room. Onboarding and mentoring are uneven. Technology and workspace investments are split, creating friction.

GitLab's Recommendation: If organizations cannot go fully remote, they should be intentional about making hybrid work equitable. Document everything, even if people are in the same office. Default to virtual meetings with everyone on their own device, even if some are co-located. Measure results, not presence. Actively combat proximity bias in promotions and opportunities. Invest equally in remote and office infrastructure.

Why GitLab Chose All-Remote: Equal playing field for all team members. Access to the best global talent without geographic constraints. Forced documentation culture leads to better institutional knowledge. Lower overhead costs. Better work-life harmony for team members. Aligned with values of transparency, efficiency, and results.

title: Engineering at GitLab
content:
The Engineering division at GitLab is responsible for building and maintaining the GitLab product. Engineering is organized into Development, Infrastructure, Quality, and Security sub-departments.

Engineering Principles: We write code that is maintainable and well-documented. We review each other's work through merge requests. We ship small, iterative changes. We invest in automated testing and CI/CD. We prioritize reliability and performance. We dog-food our own product — we use GitLab to build GitLab.

Development Process: Engineers work in monthly milestones. Work is tracked in GitLab Issues and organized on Issue Boards. Merge requests require at least one review before merging. We practice continuous deployment — changes are shipped to production frequently. Feature flags are used to gradually roll out new features.

Technical Standards: Code should be self-documenting with clear naming conventions. All changes must include appropriate tests. Performance-sensitive changes require benchmarks. Database migrations must be backward-compatible. APIs follow RESTful conventions and are versioned. Security reviews are required for sensitive changes.

Engineering Career Development: GitLab offers both individual contributor and management career tracks. Engineers can progress from Junior to Distinguished/Fellow levels. Career development conversations happen regularly in one-on-ones. We encourage engineers to contribute to open-source projects. Internal mobility between teams is supported and encouraged.

Open Source: GitLab is an open-core product. The Community Edition is fully open source. We welcome contributions from the community. Our development process is transparent — anyone can see our issues, merge requests, and roadmap. We participate in open-source events and programs.

title: Engineering Development
content:
The Development department is the largest within Engineering and is responsible for building features and maintaining the GitLab application.

Development Sub-departments: Dev (Plan, Create, Manage stages), Ops (Verify, Package, Release, Configure, Monitor stages), Sec (Secure, Govern stages), Growth, Enablement (Database, Distribution, Geo, Gitaly, Memory, and more), and ModelOps.

How Development Works: Engineers are organized into stages that align with the DevOps lifecycle. Each stage has a Product Manager, Engineering Manager, Designer, and one or more engineering teams. Work is planned in monthly milestones with priorities set collaboratively between product and engineering.

Merge Request Workflow: Open a merge request early — even in draft state. Tag reviewers and maintainers for feedback. Keep MRs small (ideally under 100 changed lines). Include tests and documentation updates. Use feature flags for significant changes. MRs are reviewed for code quality, test coverage, performance, security, and UX.

Code Review: Every merge request requires review by a domain expert. Maintainers provide final approval before merge. Reviews should be thorough but timely — we target completing reviews within two business days. Reviews focus on correctness, security, performance, readability, and alignment with architecture. We use CODEOWNERS to route reviews to the right people.

Incident Management: Development teams participate in on-call rotations for their areas. We use a defined incident management process with severity levels. Post-incident reviews (PIRs) are conducted to learn from incidents. The goal is to improve reliability through better processes and automation, not blame.

title: Quality Engineering
content:
The Quality department ensures that GitLab delivers a reliable, high-quality product experience.

Quality Engineering at GitLab: Quality Engineering focuses on testing strategy, test automation, and quality processes. Quality engineers work embedded within development teams to shift testing left and improve the overall quality culture.

Test Automation: GitLab has an extensive automated test suite covering unit, integration, and end-to-end tests. We use RSpec for backend testing and Jest for frontend testing. End-to-end tests use Capybara with Selenium. The test suite runs in CI/CD pipelines for every merge request. We aim for fast, reliable tests that give developers confidence.

Quality Processes: Bug triage happens regularly with defined severity and priority criteria. We track quality metrics including defect rates, test coverage, and mean time to resolution. We conduct quality retrospectives to continuously improve. We maintain a bug budget to balance new feature development with bug fixes.

Performance Testing: Performance is a feature at GitLab. We run performance tests as part of the CI pipeline. Reference architectures define performance expectations for different deployment sizes. We use sitespeed.io and custom tooling for frontend performance monitoring.

Testability: Quality engineers advocate for testable design. We promote test-driven development (TDD) and behavior-driven development (BDD). Documentation and test plans are part of the development process. We invest in testing infrastructure to keep the test suite fast and reliable.

title: Security at GitLab
content:
Security is a core concern at GitLab, both for the product we build and the company we operate.

Security Department: The Security department includes Application Security, Security Operations, Threat Management, Security Assurance, and Infrastructure Security teams. Security works across the entire company, embedded with engineering teams and supporting business operations.

Security Principles: Security is everyone's responsibility, not just the security team's. We practice defense in depth with multiple layers of security controls. We follow the principle of least privilege for access management. We are transparent about security issues through our public issue tracker (after patches are available). We participate in a public bug bounty program through HackerOne.

Application Security: AppSec engineers review code changes for security implications. We use SAST (Static Application Security Testing), DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing), dependency scanning, and container scanning — all built into GitLab's own CI/CD pipelines. Security reviews are required for features handling sensitive data. We follow secure coding guidelines and provide security training for all engineers.

Incident Response: We have a defined security incident response process with clear escalation paths. Security incidents are classified by severity. Post-incident reviews are conducted for all significant incidents. We maintain 24/7 security monitoring and alerting. Communication during incidents follows a defined plan including customer notification when required.

Security Compliance: GitLab maintains SOC 2 Type II certification. We comply with GDPR, CCPA, and other relevant privacy regulations. Regular security audits and penetration tests are conducted. Our compliance program covers both the product and internal company operations. We publish a Trust Center with detailed information about our security practices.

title: Product at GitLab
content:
The Product division at GitLab is responsible for defining what we build, for whom, and why.

Product Organization: Product is organized by stage groups that align with the DevOps lifecycle. Each stage group has a Product Manager who works closely with Engineering and Design. Product Managers report through a hierarchy of Group Managers, Directors, and VPs, ultimately to the Chief Product Officer.

Product Development Workflow: Product development at GitLab follows a transparent, issue-driven process. Ideas start as issues and progress through validation, design, build, and launch phases. We use a prioritization framework that balances customer needs, business goals, and technical considerations. Roadmaps are public and updated regularly.

Product Principles: We think in terms of the complete DevOps platform, not individual features. We prioritize based on customer and user impact. We dogfood our own product extensively. We iterate — shipping small, useful changes rather than waiting for large releases. We are customer-driven but not customer-controlled. We build for a global audience. We make data-informed decisions.

Categories and Maturity: GitLab's capabilities are organized into categories, each with a maturity level: Planned, Minimal, Viable, Complete, or Lovable. This framework helps communicate the current state of features to users and guides investment priorities. The Direction pages detail the vision and roadmap for each category.

User Research: Product teams conduct regular user research through surveys, interviews, usability testing, and data analysis. Research findings are shared publicly in issues. We maintain personas that represent our key user types. User feedback is a critical input to prioritization and design decisions.

Competitive Analysis: Product teams monitor the competitive landscape and maintain competitive intelligence. This information helps inform product strategy but doesn't solely drive it. We focus on building the best complete DevOps platform rather than reacting to individual competitor features.

title: Product Principles
content:
GitLab's product principles guide how we think about building software.

Single Application: GitLab provides a single application for the entire DevOps lifecycle. This is our key differentiator. A single application eliminates the complexity of integrating multiple tools, provides a consistent user experience, and enables end-to-end visibility. Every feature we build should strengthen this vision.

Convention over Configuration: We prefer sensible defaults that work for most users. When configuration is needed, make it easy. Reduce the number of decisions users need to make. Ship features that work out of the box.

Boring Solutions: We prefer simple, proven approaches over complex, novel ones. This aligns with our efficiency value. Use established patterns and technologies. Innovation should be in the user experience and capabilities, not necessarily in the underlying implementation.

Seed then Nurture: When entering a new category, start with a minimal viable feature (seed) and then iterate to maturity (nurture). Don't try to build a complete solution from day one. Ship early, get feedback, and improve.

Dogfooding: We use GitLab to build GitLab. Every team member experiences the product daily. This gives us deep empathy for our users and helps us identify issues and opportunities quickly. If a feature isn't good enough for us to use, it's not good enough for customers.

Minimum Viable Change (MVC): The smallest useful improvement we can ship. MVC is about reducing scope, not reducing quality. An MVC should solve a real problem for users, even if it doesn't solve every aspect of the larger challenge. Ship the MVC, learn from it, and iterate.

Permission to Play vs. Differentiating Features: Some features are permission to play — they're expected by users and necessary to compete. Others are differentiators that set GitLab apart. We invest in both, but we get more strategic about differentiating features.

title: UX at GitLab
content:
The UX department at GitLab ensures that the product is usable, accessible, and delightful for users.

UX Organization: The UX department includes Product Design, UX Research, and Technical Writing. Designers and researchers are embedded in stage groups, working alongside Product Managers and Engineers. The team is led by the VP of UX.

Design Process: Design at GitLab follows a collaborative, iterative approach. Designers create proposals in issues, share them for feedback, and iterate based on input from PMs, engineers, and users. We use Figma for design work and maintain a design system called Pajamas. All design work is tracked publicly in GitLab issues.

Pajamas Design System: Pajamas is GitLab's design system, providing reusable components, patterns, and guidelines. It ensures visual and interaction consistency across the product. Pajamas is publicly available and continuously evolving. All new UI work should use Pajamas components.

Accessibility: GitLab is committed to making the product accessible to all users. We target WCAG 2.1 AA compliance. Accessibility is considered from the design phase through implementation. We conduct regular accessibility audits and address issues promptly. Screen reader compatibility and keyboard navigation are priorities.

UX Research: Research is foundational to product decisions. We conduct generative research to discover user needs and evaluative research to validate solutions. Research methods include surveys, interviews, usability testing, card sorting, and tree testing. Research findings are shared publicly and contribute to prioritization.

Technical Writing: Technical writers create and maintain product documentation. Documentation is treated as a product, with its own quality standards and review process. We use a docs-as-code approach, maintaining documentation alongside the codebase. All features must include documentation as part of the definition of done.

title: Leadership at GitLab
content:
Leadership at GitLab follows our values and is adapted for the all-remote environment.

Leadership Principles: Leaders serve their teams. The best leaders remove obstacles, provide context, and create an environment where team members can do their best work. Leaders are expected to model GitLab values in all interactions. Leadership at GitLab is not about authority; it's about influence, trust, and service.

Manager Responsibilities: Managers are responsible for the performance and development of their direct reports. They conduct weekly one-on-ones, provide regular feedback, and support career growth. Managers also handle hiring, onboarding, and (when necessary) offboarding. They are expected to contribute individually as well as manage.

Skip-Level Meetings: Skip-level meetings connect team members with their manager's manager. These provide an alternate channel for feedback and ensure leadership stays connected to the work happening across the organization. Skip-levels are scheduled regularly and are open-format.

Director and Above Responsibilities: Senior leaders are responsible for strategy and cross-team alignment. They participate in key reviews and planning processes. They're expected to contribute to the handbook and share their expertise broadly. They model transparency by sharing context about decisions and trade-offs.

E-Group: GitLab's executive team (E-Group) includes the CEO and C-level executives. E-Group meetings are held weekly and key outcomes are shared broadly. E-Group members are expected to be accessible and transparent. They participate in AMA sessions and company calls.

Making Decisions: GitLab uses a DRI (Directly Responsible Individual) model for decision-making. The DRI has the authority and responsibility to make a decision. Others can provide input, but the DRI decides. This prevents decision paralysis and ensures accountability. Decisions should be documented with the reasoning behind them.

title: People Group
content:
The People Group at GitLab handles all aspects of the team member experience, from hiring through offboarding.

People Group Mission: Create an environment where everyone can thrive and do the best work of their career. The People Group covers Talent Acquisition, People Operations, Total Rewards, Learning and Development, Team Member Relations, and People Analytics.

Team Member Experience: GitLab invests in the complete team member lifecycle. This includes a structured onboarding program, ongoing development opportunities, competitive compensation, comprehensive benefits, and a supportive culture. Regular engagement surveys measure satisfaction and guide improvements.

Diversity, Inclusion and Belonging: The People Group drives initiatives to increase diversity and foster belonging. This includes diverse hiring practices, employee resource groups (TMRGs - Team Member Resource Groups), unconscious bias training, and metrics tracking. GitLab publishes its diversity data publicly.

Performance Management: GitLab uses a continuous performance management approach. Feedback is ongoing through one-on-ones and informal conversations. Formal performance reviews happen regularly with calibration across teams. Underperformance is addressed directly and supportively, with clear expectations and timelines.

Learning and Development: GitLab provides a growth and development benefit for team members to invest in their professional growth. The company supports internal mobility, role changes, and skill development. Leadership development programs are available for managers and aspiring leaders. We encourage team members to spend time learning and sharing knowledge.

Total Rewards: Compensation at GitLab is based on a transparent compensation calculator that factors in role, level, experience, and location. Benefits include equity (stock options), comprehensive health coverage, flexible PTO, parental leave, and home office setup stipends. Total rewards information is publicly available in the handbook.

title: Hiring at GitLab
content:
GitLab's hiring process is designed to be fair, efficient, and aligned with our values.

Hiring Philosophy: We hire the best talent globally, regardless of location. We look for people who align with our values and can thrive in an all-remote environment. We believe in diversity and actively work to build diverse teams. We prioritize skills and potential over specific credentials.

Job Families: GitLab defines roles through job families that outline responsibilities, requirements, and career progression. Job families are publicly available in the handbook, providing transparency about what's expected at each level. This helps candidates understand what they're applying for and helps team members plan their careers.

Interview Process: The standard interview process includes: application review, screening call with a recruiter, technical assessment or take-home exercise (role dependent), team interviews (typically 2-4), and a final decision. The entire process is documented in the handbook. Interviewers use structured scorecards to ensure consistency and reduce bias.

Candidate Experience: We aim to make the hiring process transparent and respectful. Candidates receive timely updates. Rejection feedback is provided when possible. We publish our interview process publicly so candidates can prepare. We respect candidates' time and keep the process as efficient as possible.

Referral Program: GitLab has a referral bonus program encouraging team members to refer talented people from their networks. Referrals are a valuable hiring channel because referred candidates often align well with company culture.

Intern Program: GitLab runs intern programs that provide meaningful work experience. Interns are treated as team members and are expected to contribute to real projects. Successful interns may receive full-time offers.

title: Interviewing at GitLab
content:
Detailed guidelines for the GitLab interview process.

Interview Structure: Interviews at GitLab are competency-based, focusing on past behavior and demonstrated skills. We use structured interviews with predefined questions to ensure consistency across candidates. Interviewers assess candidates against the job requirements and GitLab values.

Technical Interviews: For technical roles, candidates may complete a take-home assignment, live coding exercise, or architecture discussion depending on the role. The goal is to assess real-world problem-solving ability, not trick questions. Candidates are evaluated on code quality, communication, problem-solving approach, and technical depth.

Values Interview: Every candidate goes through a values interview that assesses alignment with GitLab's CREDIT values. This is a behavioral interview where candidates share examples of how they've demonstrated collaboration, results-orientation, efficiency, inclusion, iteration, and transparency in their work.

Interviewer Training: All interviewers at GitLab complete training on structured interviewing, unconscious bias, and using the evaluation rubric. We calibrate regularly to ensure interviewers are aligned on expectations. Interviewers are expected to complete their scorecards promptly after each interview.

Scorecard System: After each interview, the interviewer completes a scorecard with ratings and detailed comments. Scorecards are reviewed during the hiring decision meeting. The focus is on evidence-based assessment rather than gut feeling. Hiring decisions are made collaboratively by the interview panel.

Remote Interview Best Practices: All interviews are conducted over video call. Ensure a quiet environment and stable internet connection. Test technology before the interview. Be mindful of time zones. Allow time for candidates to ask questions. Make candidates comfortable — remember, they're evaluating us too.

title: Onboarding at GitLab
content:
GitLab's onboarding process is designed to help new team members become effective and feel welcome in our all-remote environment.

Onboarding Structure: New team members start with a structured onboarding issue that guides them through their first weeks. The issue contains tasks for setting up tools, completing training, meeting key people, and learning about GitLab's culture and processes. Onboarding is self-paced but has suggested timelines.

Onboarding Buddy: Every new hire is paired with an onboarding buddy — an experienced team member from a different team. The buddy helps answer questions, provides context about company culture, and serves as an informal resource. Buddies and new hires schedule regular check-ins during the first few months.

Tools and Access: New team members receive a laptop and setup stipend before their start date. Access to GitLab, Slack, Google Workspace, and other tools is provisioned through automated workflows. IT support is available to help with any setup issues.

First Week: The first week focuses on completing essential setup tasks, meeting the team, attending onboarding sessions, and getting oriented with the handbook. New hires are encouraged to make their first merge request to the handbook during the first week — even if it's just fixing a typo.

First Month: The first month expands to understanding team-specific processes, contributing to real work, building relationships through coffee chats, and completing required training. Managers check in regularly to ensure the new hire is settling in.

First 90 Days: By the end of the first 90 days, team members should be fully productive in their role, have a good understanding of GitLab's culture and values, and have established relationships across the company. A 90-day check-in with the manager reviews progress and sets goals for the next period.

Continuous Improvement: GitLab continuously improves its onboarding process based on feedback from new hires. Every new team member is encouraged to suggest improvements to the onboarding issue and handbook pages they encounter.

title: TeamOps
content:
TeamOps is GitLab's framework for optimizing team operations and decision-making.

What is TeamOps: TeamOps is a set of principles and practices that GitLab has developed through years of operating as an all-remote company. It's designed to optimize information management, decision-making, and team execution. TeamOps is publicly documented and offered as a certification program.

Core Tenets: TeamOps is built on four key tenets: Shared Reality (ensuring everyone has access to the same information), Equal Contributions (enabling everyone to participate regardless of role, location, or timezone), Decision Velocity (making decisions quickly and effectively), and Measurement Clarity (tracking outcomes to learn and improve).

Shared Reality: All team members should have access to the same information. This means documenting everything in a single source of truth (the handbook), recording meetings, sharing context broadly, and avoiding information silos. When everyone operates from the same information, better decisions follow.

Equal Contributions: Everyone's input should be valued regardless of their position, location, or timezone. Asynchronous communication enables this by giving everyone time to contribute thoughtfully. Meetings should be designed to hear from all participants, not just the loudest voices.

Decision Velocity: Fast, good decisions beat slow, perfect decisions. Use the DRI model to empower individuals to make decisions. Gather input efficiently, make the decision, document it, and move forward. Decisions can be revisited if new information emerges.

Measurement Clarity: Define clear metrics for success before starting work. Measure outcomes, not activities. Share results transparently. Use data to inform future decisions. Celebrate what's working and learn from what isn't.

TeamOps Certification: GitLab offers a TeamOps certification program that teaches these principles to individuals and organizations. The certification covers remote work best practices, asynchronous communication, and collaborative decision-making.

title: GitLab Direction Overview
content:
GitLab's product direction outlines where the product is headed and the vision for the complete DevOps platform.

Vision: GitLab's vision is to be the leading complete DevOps platform, delivered as a single application, built on open source. We believe that a single application for the entire software development lifecycle provides the best experience for developers and organizations.

Strategy: Our strategy focuses on expanding the capabilities of the GitLab platform across all stages of the DevOps lifecycle while deepening the value of having everything in one application. Key strategic themes include: AI-powered software development, comprehensive security and compliance, cloud-native development support, and enterprise scalability.

DevOps Stages: GitLab covers the complete DevOps lifecycle: Plan (project management, issue tracking), Create (source code management, code review, wiki), Verify (continuous integration, testing), Package (package registry, container registry), Secure (SAST, DAST, dependency scanning), Govern (compliance, audit, policies), Release (continuous delivery, feature flags), Configure (infrastructure as code, Kubernetes), Monitor (incident management, error tracking), and ModelOps (ML model development, data science).

AI Integration: GitLab is investing heavily in AI capabilities across the platform. This includes AI-powered code suggestions, automated code review, natural language to code generation, intelligent test generation, security vulnerability remediation, and AI-driven insights across the development lifecycle. The goal is to make every developer more productive.

Open Core Model: GitLab uses an open-core business model. The Community Edition (CE) is freely available and open source. The Enterprise Edition (EE) adds additional features for larger organizations. Features are placed in the appropriate tier based on the buyer persona (individual contributors vs. directors/executives).

Competitive Landscape: GitLab competes with specialized tools across each DevOps stage. Our competitive advantage is the single-application approach that eliminates tool chain complexity and provides end-to-end visibility. We also compete with other platform offerings from major cloud providers.

Customer-Driven Development: Product direction is heavily influenced by customer feedback, usage data, and market analysis. We balance the needs of our diverse user base (individual developers, teams, enterprises) with our strategic vision. Community contributions also shape the product direction.

title: Category Maturity
content:
GitLab uses a maturity model to communicate the current state and trajectory of each product category.

Maturity Levels: Planned — the feature or category is on the roadmap but not yet started. Minimal — basic functionality exists that solves the core problem in a limited way. Viable — the feature meets the needs of most users for standard use cases. Complete — the feature is competitive with best-in-class standalone tools for the category. Lovable — the feature exceeds expectations and users are passionate advocates.

How Maturity is Determined: Maturity is assessed based on feature completeness, user feedback, competitive comparison, and adoption metrics. Product Managers evaluate maturity regularly and update the direction pages. Moving up in maturity requires sustained investment and user validation.

Maturity by Stage: Each DevOps stage contains multiple categories with varying maturity levels. Some categories like Source Code Management, Continuous Integration, and Issue Tracking are at the Complete or Lovable level. Others like ModelOps and some Govern categories are at earlier maturity levels. The direction pages show the current maturity and planned improvements for each category.

Investment Priorities: GitLab prioritizes investment based on a combination of maturity gaps, customer demand, competitive pressure, and strategic importance. Categories at lower maturity levels that are strategically important receive focused investment to move them up the maturity ladder.

Transparency: The maturity model is publicly visible on GitLab's direction pages. This transparency helps customers understand what to expect and helps the community focus contributions where they'll have the most impact. We update maturity assessments regularly and welcome feedback.

title: Plan Stage Direction
content:
The Plan stage in GitLab provides tools for project management, issue tracking, and portfolio planning.

Vision: Make GitLab the best place to plan and manage software development work, from individual tasks to enterprise portfolios. Integrate planning tightly with execution so teams have real-time visibility into progress.

Key Categories: Issue Tracking — create, assign, label, and track issues through their lifecycle. Boards — Kanban-style boards for visualizing and managing work. Epics — group related issues across projects for larger initiatives. Roadmaps — visualize planned and in-progress work on a timeline. Requirements Management — define and track requirements alongside development work. Service Desk — receive and manage external requests within GitLab.

Recent Improvements: Enhanced iteration and milestone planning. Better epic hierarchy and cross-project coordination. Improved boards with more filtering and swimlane options. Markdown and rich text improvements in issues. Better time tracking and estimation features.

Future Direction: AI-powered project insights and recommendations. Advanced portfolio management for enterprise customers. Tighter integration between planning and execution data. Better support for scaled agile frameworks. Improved reporting and analytics. Enhanced work item types for flexibility.

Competitive Positioning: GitLab's planning capabilities compete with tools like Jira, Asana, and Linear. Our advantage is that planning is integrated with the development workflow, eliminating context switching and providing accurate, real-time status based on actual code changes and deployments.

title: Create Stage Direction
content:
The Create stage covers source code management, code review, and collaborative coding tools.

Vision: Provide the most productive environment for writing, reviewing, and managing code. Leverage AI to accelerate development while maintaining code quality and security.

Key Categories: Source Code Management — Git repository hosting with branching, tagging, and access controls. Code Review — merge request workflow with inline comments, approvals, and suggestions. Web IDE — browser-based code editing integrated into the GitLab workflow. Snippets — share and collaborate on code fragments. Wiki — documentation alongside the codebase.

AI-Powered Development: Code Suggestions powered by AI provide real-time code completion and generation. AI-assisted code review helps reviewers focus on important aspects. Natural language can be used to generate code, tests, and documentation. AI helps explain complex code and suggest refactoring improvements.

Recent Improvements: Enhanced merge request experience with better performance and usability. Improved diff rendering for large changes. Better branch management and protected branch rules. Enhanced Web IDE with VS Code-based editing. Improved Git performance for large repositories.

Future Direction: Deeper AI integration throughout the coding workflow. Collaborative real-time editing. Enhanced code search and navigation. Better support for monorepos. Improved merge conflict resolution. Advanced code intelligence features.

Competitive Positioning: GitLab's Create stage competes with GitHub, Bitbucket, and specialized code review tools. Our advantage is the seamless integration with CI/CD, security scanning, and project management, providing a complete workflow without leaving the platform.

title: Verify Stage Direction
content:
The Verify stage provides continuous integration and testing capabilities.

Vision: Make CI/CD pipelines fast, reliable, and easy to configure. Enable teams to verify code changes quickly and confidently.

Key Categories: Continuous Integration — automated build and test pipelines triggered by code changes. Pipeline Authoring — YAML-based pipeline configuration with templates and includes. Testing — integrated test execution, reporting, and coverage tracking. Runner — GitLab's execution engine for running CI/CD jobs on various platforms. Pipeline Execution — job scheduling, parallelization, and resource management.

Recent Improvements: Faster pipeline execution with better parallelization. Improved pipeline editor with syntax validation and visualization. Better caching and artifact management. Enhanced runner autoscaling on cloud platforms. Improved test reporting with flaky test detection.

Future Direction: AI-generated pipeline configurations. Intelligent test selection to run only relevant tests. Faster feedback loops through optimized pipeline execution. Better pipeline analytics and insights. Enhanced support for mobile and embedded development. Improved pipeline reliability and debugging tools.

Competitive Positioning: GitLab CI/CD is recognized as a leader in the space, competing with Jenkins, GitHub Actions, CircleCI, and cloud-provider CI/CD services. Our advantage is native integration with the rest of the GitLab platform and a powerful, flexible pipeline configuration language.

title: Secure Stage Direction
content:
The Secure stage integrates security testing directly into the development workflow.

Vision: Shift security left by making it a natural part of the development process. Enable developers to find and fix security issues before they reach production.

Key Categories: SAST (Static Application Security Testing) — analyze source code for vulnerabilities. DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing) — test running applications for security issues. Dependency Scanning — identify vulnerabilities in project dependencies. Container Scanning — scan container images for known vulnerabilities. Secret Detection — prevent secrets and credentials from being committed to repositories. License Compliance — track and enforce open-source license policies. Fuzz Testing — automated fuzzing to discover unknown vulnerabilities.

Recent Improvements: More accurate and faster scanners with fewer false positives. Better vulnerability management workflow. Enhanced security dashboards and reporting. Improved integration with merge request workflow. AI-assisted vulnerability remediation suggestions.

Future Direction: AI-powered vulnerability prioritization and remediation. Expanded language and framework coverage for all scanners. Better integration with external security tools. Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) support. Enhanced policy management for security requirements. Continuous security monitoring beyond the CI pipeline.

Competitive Positioning: GitLab's security scanning competes with Snyk, Checkmarx, Veracode, and other application security tools. Our advantage is that security is built into the platform and runs automatically as part of the development workflow, rather than being a separate tool that developers need to adopt.

title: Govern Stage Direction
content:
The Govern stage provides compliance, audit, and policy management capabilities.

Vision: Enable organizations to meet compliance requirements without slowing down development. Make governance a natural part of the development workflow rather than an afterthought.

Key Categories: Compliance Management — enforce and track compliance across projects and groups. Audit Events — comprehensive logging of actions and changes for audit trails. Security Policies — define and enforce security and compliance policies. Vulnerability Management — track, triage, and remediate security vulnerabilities. Dependency Management — manage and govern dependencies across projects.

Recent Improvements: Enhanced compliance frameworks with customizable requirements. Better audit logging with more detailed events. Improved policy management with merge request approval policies. Enhanced vulnerability reporting and dashboards. Software composition analysis improvements.

Future Direction: AI-powered compliance recommendations. Advanced policy-as-code capabilities. Better integration with external GRC (Governance, Risk, and Compliance) tools. Enhanced SBOM management. Automated compliance reporting. Broader regulatory framework support.

Competitive Positioning: GitLab's governance capabilities compete with specialized GRC tools and other DevSecOps platforms. Our advantage is that governance is integrated into the development workflow, providing real-time compliance visibility rather than point-in-time assessments.

title: Release Stage Direction
content:
The Release stage covers continuous delivery and deployment capabilities.

Vision: Make deployments boring through automation, progressive delivery, and comprehensive rollback capabilities.

Key Categories: Continuous Delivery — automated deployment pipelines to any environment. Feature Flags — control feature visibility without redeployment. Release Orchestration — coordinate complex multi-service releases. Pages — static site hosting directly from GitLab repositories. Environments — manage and track deployment environments.

Recent Improvements: Enhanced feature flags with more targeting options. Better environment management and deployment tracking. Improved release evidence and changelog generation. Enhanced integration with Kubernetes for deployments. Better rollback and recovery mechanisms.

Future Direction: AI-assisted release management and risk assessment. Advanced progressive delivery with canary and blue-green deployments. Better multi-cloud deployment support. Enhanced release analytics and insights. Improved deployment approval workflows. GitOps-first delivery patterns.

Competitive Positioning: GitLab's release capabilities compete with LaunchDarkly (feature flags), Harness, and ArgoCD. Our advantage is tight integration with the CI pipeline and the rest of the development lifecycle, providing end-to-end traceability from issue to deployment.

title: Monitor Stage Direction
content:
The Monitor stage provides observability and incident management capabilities.

Vision: Close the DevOps loop by connecting production insights back to development. Enable teams to detect, respond to, and learn from production issues quickly.

Key Categories: Incident Management — detect, triage, and resolve production incidents. Error Tracking — capture, group, and track application errors. On-Call Management — manage on-call schedules and escalation policies. Status Pages — communicate service status to stakeholders. Service Desk — manage inbound requests and incidents from external users.

Recent Improvements: Enhanced incident management workflow with better timeline tracking. Improved error tracking with better grouping and deduplication. Better integration with alerting tools (PagerDuty, OpsGenie). Enhanced on-call management features. Improved status page functionality.

Future Direction: AI-powered root cause analysis. Tighter integration with external observability tools. Better connection between incidents and code changes. Enhanced runbook automation. Improved service dependency mapping. Advanced incident analytics and metrics.

Competitive Positioning: GitLab's monitoring capabilities are still maturing compared to specialized tools like Datadog, PagerDuty, and ServiceNow. Our advantage is the connection between production issues and the code, enabling faster root cause identification and resolution.

title: Compensation at GitLab
content:
GitLab's compensation philosophy is built on transparency and fairness.

Compensation Calculator: GitLab uses a transparent compensation calculator that determines pay based on role, level, experience factor, and location factor. The calculator is publicly available, meaning anyone can see how compensation is determined. This transparency helps eliminate pay gaps and ensures fairness.

Location Factor: GitLab pays based on the cost of market in the team member's location. The location factor is a multiplier applied to a benchmark salary. This means the same role at the same level may have different pay in different locations, reflecting local market conditions. We use a rent index as part of the location factor calculation.

Role and Level: Each role at GitLab has defined levels (Junior, Intermediate, Senior, Staff, Principal, Distinguished) with corresponding compensation bands. The level is determined by the responsibilities and expectations outlined in the job family.

Experience Factor: Within each level, the experience factor adjusts compensation based on relevant experience and tenure. This allows for pay progression within a level.

Equity: GitLab provides stock options as part of the total compensation package. All team members receive equity grants. Refresh grants are available for continued service and exceptional performance. The equity program is designed to align team member interests with company success.

Pay Transparency: GitLab's approach to pay transparency is unique and deliberate. By making the compensation formula public, we reduce negotiation bias, build trust, and help team members understand their total rewards. This aligns with our value of transparency.

title: Benefits at GitLab
content:
GitLab offers comprehensive benefits to support team members' health, wellbeing, and financial security.

General Benefits: All GitLab team members worldwide have access to a core set of benefits, with additional country-specific benefits where applicable.

Flexible PTO: GitLab has a flexible paid time off policy. Team members are encouraged to take time off when they need it without a set number of days. Managers support team members in taking adequate rest. The minimum expected is 25 days per year. We track time off to ensure people are taking enough rest.

Growth and Development Benefit: A yearly stipend for professional development, including courses, certifications, conferences, coaching, and books. This supports our value of investing in team member growth.

Home Office Setup: New team members receive a stipend for setting up their home office. This can be used for a desk, chair, monitor, and other ergonomic equipment. There is also an ongoing stipend for internet and coworking space costs.

Health and Wellness: Comprehensive health insurance is provided in most countries. Mental health support through Modern Health includes therapy sessions and coaching. Wellness stipends cover gym memberships, fitness classes, and wellness activities.

Parental Leave: GitLab provides generous parental leave for all parents, regardless of gender. The policy covers birth, adoption, and foster care. Return-to-work support is available.

Travel: While GitLab is all-remote, team members may travel for company events (like Contribute), team onsites, or customer meetings. Travel expenses are covered according to the travel policy.

Other Benefits: Life insurance, disability coverage, employee assistance programs (EAP), and retirement plan matching are available in many countries. Specific benefits vary by location due to local laws and practices.

title: Sales at GitLab
content:
The Sales organization at GitLab is responsible for revenue growth and customer relationships.

Sales Organization: Sales is organized into segments: Enterprise, Mid-Market, and SMB (Small and Medium Business). Each segment has dedicated sales teams, including Account Executives, Solution Architects, and Customer Success Managers. The organization also includes Sales Development Representatives (SDRs) who generate qualified leads.

Sales Process: GitLab follows a defined sales methodology with clear stages from lead qualification to closed deal. The process is documented in the handbook and supported by Salesforce CRM. Solution Architects provide technical expertise during complex sales cycles. Proposals and pricing follow established guidelines.

Value Selling: GitLab sales focuses on the value of the complete DevOps platform. Key value propositions include: reduced tool chain complexity, improved developer productivity, faster time to market, built-in security and compliance, and lower total cost of ownership.

Channel Partners: GitLab works with technology partners and resellers to extend market reach. The channel program provides partners with training, resources, and incentives. Partners help customers implement and optimize their GitLab usage.

Customer Success: Customer Success Managers (CSMs) work with customers post-sale to ensure they realize value from GitLab. CSMs help with adoption, expansion, and renewal. They gather customer feedback and advocate for customer needs internally. The goal is to create long-term, successful partnerships.

Sales Handbook: The sales handbook contains detailed playbooks, competitive information, pricing guidelines, and process documentation. It is continuously updated to reflect the latest strategies and market conditions.

title: Marketing at GitLab
content:
GitLab's Marketing organization drives awareness, demand generation, and community engagement.

Marketing Organization: Marketing includes Brand, Product Marketing, Content, Digital Experience, Community, Developer Relations, Corporate Communications, and Demand Generation teams. Each team plays a role in reaching and engaging GitLab's target audiences.

Product Marketing: Product Marketing positions GitLab in the market, develops messaging, creates competitive analysis, and supports sales with enablement materials. PMMs work closely with Product Management to bring new features to market effectively.

Community and Developer Relations: GitLab has a strong open-source community. Developer Relations engages with the broader developer community through events, content, and programs. The GitLab Heroes program recognizes community contributors. Community contributions to the codebase are actively encouraged and supported.

Content Marketing: Content creation follows an educational, transparent approach aligned with GitLab values. The blog covers technical topics, company updates, and thought leadership. Case studies and customer stories demonstrate real-world GitLab usage. All content aims to be helpful and authentic rather than promotional.

Demand Generation: Demand generation activities include digital advertising, events, webinars, content syndication, and partner marketing. The team focuses on generating qualified leads and supporting pipeline growth. All activities are measured and optimized based on data.

Brand: GitLab's brand identity reflects its values of transparency, collaboration, and openness. The tanuki (raccoon dog) mascot is a beloved symbol of the company. Brand guidelines ensure consistency across all touchpoints.

title: Finance at GitLab
content:
The Finance team manages GitLab's financial operations, planning, and strategy.

Finance Organization: Finance includes Accounting, Financial Planning & Analysis (FP&A), Tax, Treasury, Procurement, and Business Operations. The team supports data-driven decision-making across the company.

Financial Planning: GitLab follows a structured planning process with annual plans, quarterly updates, and monthly business reviews. Financial targets and actuals are shared with team members as part of our transparency value. The planning process is collaborative, involving input from all departments.

Procurement: The procurement process ensures that company spending is efficient and compliant. All significant purchases go through a defined approval process. Vendors are evaluated based on security, cost, and alignment with GitLab's needs. The procurement handbook provides clear guidelines for team members.

Expense Management: Team members follow the expense policy for business-related spending. The policy emphasizes spending company money as if it were your own (aligned with the efficiency value). Receipts are required and expenses are reviewed regularly. Common expenses include travel, home office equipment, and professional development.

As a public company: GitLab (NASDAQ: GTLB) is publicly traded. This brings additional financial reporting requirements, insider trading policies, and communication guidelines. Quarterly earnings calls and SEC filings provide transparency to investors and the public.

title: Legal at GitLab
content:
The Legal team at GitLab handles corporate legal matters, contracts, intellectual property, and compliance.

Legal Organization: The Legal team includes Corporate Legal, Commercial Legal (contracts and deals), Employment Legal, Privacy, and Legal Operations. The team supports all departments and ensures GitLab operates within legal requirements.

Contracts and Agreements: Commercial Legal reviews and negotiates customer contracts, vendor agreements, and partnership deals. Standard terms and templates are available for common agreement types. Non-standard terms require legal review.

Intellectual Property: GitLab's open-source code is licensed under MIT (Community Edition) and proprietary licenses (Enterprise Edition). The Legal team manages IP protection, trademark enforcement, and open-source compliance. Contributors sign a Contributor License Agreement (CLA).

Privacy: GitLab is committed to protecting user and team member privacy. We comply with GDPR, CCPA, and other privacy regulations. The Privacy team conducts data protection impact assessments and maintains our privacy documentation. Our privacy practices are documented publicly.

Employment Law: Employment Legal ensures compliance with labor laws across all countries where GitLab has team members. This is complex given our presence in 65+ countries with different employment regulations. We use Professional Employer Organizations (PEOs) and entities in various countries to ensure compliance.

Code of Conduct: GitLab maintains a code of business conduct and ethics that applies to all team members. The code covers conflicts of interest, anti-corruption, insider trading, and professional behavior. Training is required and compliance is monitored.

title: IT at GitLab
content:
The IT department at GitLab supports team members with technology, tools, and infrastructure.

IT Organization: IT includes End User Services (helpdesk and support), IT Engineering (automation and infrastructure), IT Security (endpoint security and access management), and IT Compliance. The team serves all GitLab team members worldwide.

Tools and Platforms: GitLab team members use a standard set of tools: GitLab (obviously), Google Workspace, Slack, Zoom, Okta (SSO), and various department-specific tools. IT manages provisioning, licensing, and support for all tools. New tool requests go through an evaluation process.

Laptop Management: All team members receive a company-provisioned laptop. IT manages device security, updates, and compliance. Lost or stolen devices are handled through remote wipe capabilities. Laptop refreshes happen on a regular cycle.

Access Management: Access to tools and systems follows the principle of least privilege. Okta provides single sign-on and multi-factor authentication. Access requests are handled through GitLab issues. Regular access reviews ensure appropriate access levels are maintained.

IT Support: IT support is available globally through Slack channels and a helpdesk system. Common issues are documented in the handbook for self-service resolution. IT aims for quick response times and high satisfaction ratings.

Security Practices: Endpoint detection and response (EDR) is deployed on all company devices. All connections to company resources require VPN or zero-trust network access. Regular security awareness training is required for all team members. Phishing simulations test and improve team member awareness.

title: Infrastructure at GitLab
content:
The Infrastructure department ensures that GitLab.com and self-managed GitLab instances run reliably and efficiently.

Infrastructure Organization: Infrastructure includes Reliability Engineering, Delivery, Scalability, and Database Reliability teams. The department is responsible for keeping GitLab.com available, performant, and scalable.

GitLab.com Reliability: GitLab.com serves millions of users and repositories. The Reliability team manages uptime through proactive monitoring, capacity planning, and incident response. We target high availability and publish our uptime statistics publicly. Service Level Objectives (SLOs) guide operational priorities.

Delivery: The Delivery team manages the release process for both GitLab.com and self-managed GitLab. Monthly releases follow a predictable schedule. Security patches are released as needed. The delivery pipeline is heavily automated.

Scalability: As GitLab.com grows, the Scalability team ensures the platform handles increasing load. This involves architecture improvements, performance optimization, and capacity planning. Scalability challenges and solutions are documented publicly.

Database Reliability: The Database Reliability team manages the PostgreSQL databases that power GitLab.com. This includes performance tuning, migration management, backup and recovery, and scaling strategies. Database changes are carefully reviewed for performance impact.

Reference Architectures: GitLab provides reference architectures for self-managed deployments at various scales. These architectures are tested and validated to meet specific performance requirements. They guide customers in deploying GitLab optimally for their needs.

Incident Management: Infrastructure follows GitLab's incident management process with defined severity levels and response procedures. Post-incident reviews are conducted publicly to improve processes and share learning. The team maintains on-call rotations for 24/7 coverage.

title: GitLab Company Overview
content:
GitLab Inc. is a company that provides a complete DevOps platform delivered as a single application.

Mission: Everyone can contribute. GitLab's mission is to make it so that everyone can contribute to software creation, regardless of their background, role, or location. This applies to our product (open source, collaborative), our company (all-remote, transparent), and our community (inclusive, welcoming).

History: GitLab was founded in 2011 by Dmitriy Zaporozhets and Sid Sijbrandij. Dmitriy created the initial GitLab project as an open-source Git repository manager. Sid joined as a contributor and later became CEO. The company grew rapidly, embracing an all-remote model from the beginning. GitLab went public on NASDAQ (GTLB) in October 2021.

Product: GitLab provides a complete DevOps platform in a single application. This includes project planning, source code management, CI/CD, security testing, monitoring, and more. The product is available in multiple tiers: Free (open source Community Edition), Premium, and Ultimate. GitLab can be self-managed or used as a SaaS service (GitLab.com).

Company Size: GitLab has over 2,000 team members in 65+ countries. There are no company-owned offices. Team members work from home, coworking spaces, or wherever they choose. The company has a market capitalization of several billion dollars.

Culture: GitLab's culture is defined by its CREDIT values and all-remote approach. The company is radically transparent, with its handbook, OKRs, and many internal processes publicly visible. This transparency has made GitLab a thought leader in remote work and open company practices.

Customers: GitLab is used by organizations of all sizes, from individual developers to Fortune 500 enterprises. Notable customers include Goldman Sachs, Siemens, NVIDIA, T-Mobile, and many government agencies. The platform supports millions of users worldwide.
